items.title: Prevalence and Importance of Addressing Sexual Dysfunction in Clinical Practice
items.content: Male sexual dysfunction affects up to 31% of middle-aged and elderly men, while female sexual dysfunction has a higher prevalence at 43%. Given the significant impact on quality of life, physicians should proactively address sexual health during clinical encounters, especially for patients with cardiovascular risk factors.
keyPoints: Prevalence of male sexual dysfunction: up to 31% in middle-aged and elderly men
Prevalence of female sexual dysfunction: 43%
Sexual health is a crucial aspect of quality of life
Routine discussion of sexual health should be part of clinical encounters, especially for those with cardiovascular risk factors

items.title: Impact of Demographic Changes and Awareness on Sexual Dysfunction Diagnosis
items.content: Increased diagnosis and healthcare expenditures for sexual dysfunction are driven by demographic changes, the popularity of newer treatments, and greater societal awareness. Physicians should directly address sexual health to uncover issues that patients might be reluctant to discuss.
keyPoints: Demographic changes contribute to increased diagnosis of sexual dysfunction
Newer treatments have gained popularity, impacting healthcare expenditures
Greater societal awareness has led to more diagnoses
Physicians should proactively inquire about sexual health due to patient reluctance

items.title: Mechanism of Penile Erection and Role of Nitric Oxide
items.content: Penile erection is a neurovascular event requiring intact cardiovascular function. It involves increased blood flow into the lacunar network, relaxation of arteries and corporal smooth muscle, and venous occlusion leading to a rigid erection. Nitric oxide plays a crucial role by inducing vascular relaxation through cyclic GMP production.
keyPoints: Erection is dependent on neurovascular events and cardiovascular integrity.
Nitric oxide induces vascular relaxation via cyclic GMP.
PDE-5 inhibitors like sildenafil maintain erections by reducing cyclic GMP breakdown.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · PHYSIOLOGY OF MALE SEXUAL RESPONSE

items.title: Ejaculation Process and Control Mechanisms
items.content: Ejaculation is controlled by the sympathetic nervous system, involving contraction of reproductive organs to propel seminal fluid into the urethra. It includes emission followed by expulsion, coordinated by autonomic and somatic spinal centers.
keyPoints: Sympathetic nervous system stimulates ejaculation.
Emission involves contractions of epididymis, vas deferens, seminal vesicles, and prostate.
Expulsion is characterized by rhythmic contractions of perineal muscles.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · PHYSIOLOGY OF MALE SEXUAL RESPONSE

items.title: Detumescence Mechanism and Causes of Premature Detumescence
items.content: Detumescence is mediated by norepinephrine, endothelin, and smooth-muscle contraction via $\alpha$-adrenergic receptors. Venous leak can cause premature detumescence due to insufficient relaxation of corporal smooth muscle.
keyPoints: Detumescence involves norepinephrine and endothelin.
Venous leak causes premature detumescence, not specific anatomic defects.
Priapism is a persistent erection linked to conditions like sickle cell anemia.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · PHYSIOLOGY OF MALE SEXUAL RESPONSE

items.title: Epidemiology and Risk Factors for Erectile Dysfunction
items.content: Erectile dysfunction is not a normal part of aging but is associated with age-related physiological and psychological changes. The Massachusetts Male Aging Study reported that 52% of men aged 40–70 experienced some degree of ED, with complete ED in 10%. Incidence increases significantly with age, especially among those aged 50-59. Risk factors include diabetes mellitus, obesity, cardiovascular disease, smoking, certain medications, and psychological issues.
keyPoints: 52% of men aged 40–70 report some degree of ED (Massachusetts Male Aging Study).
Complete ED occurs in 10%, moderate in 25%, minimal in 17%.
Incidence more than doubles between ages 40 and 70.
Highest incidence among men aged 50-59 (21%) and those who are poor, divorced, or less educated.
ED is higher in men with diabetes, obesity, cardiovascular disease, hypertension, low HDL levels, and systemic inflammation.
Smoking and certain medications for diabetes/cardiovascular diseases are significant risk factors.

items.title: Pathophysiology of Erectile Dysfunction
items.content: Erectile dysfunction can result from psychogenic, endocrinologic, neurogenic, arteriogenic, or veno-occlusive mechanisms. Over 80% of ED cases in older men are due to diabetic, atherosclerotic, and drug-related causes. Vasculogenic issues, such as decreased blood flow or excessive outflow, are common organic causes. Neurogenic disorders affecting the sacral spinal cord or autonomic fibers can also lead to ED.
keyPoints: ED mechanisms: psychogenic, endocrinologic, neurogenic, arteriogenic, veno-occlusive.
>80% of ED in older men due to diabetic, atherosclerotic, and drug-related causes.
Vasculogenic issues are the most common organic cause.
Neurogenic disorders affecting sacral spinal cord or autonomic fibers lead to ED.

items.title: Endocrinologic Factors in Erectile Dysfunction
items.content: Androgens, particularly testosterone, play a role in erectile function by optimizing nitric oxide synthase and the nitric oxide cascade. While men with castrate levels of testosterone can still achieve erections from stimuli, normal testosterone levels are important for optimal function. Androgen replacement therapy is beneficial only when ED is secondary to hypogonadism. Hyperprolactinemia can decrease libido by reducing testosterone levels, but treatment with dopamine agonists can restore it.
keyPoints: Normal testosterone levels optimize nitric oxide synthase and the nitric oxide cascade for erectile function.
Androgen replacement therapy improves ED only when secondary to hypogonadism.
Hyperprolactinemia decreases libido by suppressing GnRH, reducing testosterone levels.
Treatment of hyperprolactinemia with dopamine agonists can restore libido.

items.title: Psychogenic Erectile Dysfunction: Mechanisms and Common Causes
items.content: Psychogenic erectile dysfunction involves two primary mechanisms. First, psychogenic stimuli may inhibit reflexogenic responses by blocking vasodilator outflow to the penis through sacral cord inhibition. Second, increased sympathetic stimulation due to anxiety can elevate penile smooth-muscle tone. Common causes include performance anxiety, depression, relationship conflict, loss of attraction, sexual inhibition, conflicts over sexual preference, childhood sexual abuse, and fear of pregnancy or sexually transmitted diseases.
keyPoints: Psychogenic stimuli inhibit reflexogenic responses by blocking vasodilator outflow to the penis.
Excess sympathetic stimulation increases penile smooth-muscle tone.

Common causes: performance anxiety, depression, relationship conflict, loss of attraction, sexual inhibition, conflicts over sexual preference, childhood sexual abuse, fear of pregnancy or sexually transmitted diseases.

items.title: Medication-Related Erectile Dysfunction: Prevalence and Mechanisms
items.content: Medication-induced erectile dysfunction is estimated to occur in 25% of men seen in general medical clinics, with effects being additive, especially in older men. Antihypertensive agents like thiazide diuretics and beta blockers are frequently implicated, while calcium channel blockers and ACE inhibitors are cited less often. These medications may affect erectile function directly or indirectly by reducing pelvic blood pressure. SSRIs, particularly paroxetine and escitalopram, have a higher risk of causing sexual dysfunction due to their inhibition of nitric oxide synthase and serotonin's role in decreasing dopamine-enhanced libido.
keyPoints: Medication-induced ED occurs in 25% of men seen in general medical clinics.
Thiazide diuretics and beta blockers are most frequently implicated among antihypertensives.
SSRIs, especially paroxetine and escitalopram, have a higher risk for sexual dysfunction.
Serotonin inhibits normal sexual response by decreasing dopamine-enhanced libido and increasing prolactin release.

items.title: Initiating Discussion on ED
items.content: A primary care provider is well-suited to initiate discussions about erectile dysfunction due to the sensitive nature of the topic. Patients are more likely to disclose their issues once the physician brings up the subject.
keyPoints: Primary care providers often best initiate ED evaluation.
Patient embarrassment and perceived inattention by physicians lead to underreporting.
Initiating conversation increases patient willingness to discuss potency issues.

items.title: Assessing Causes of ED
items.content: A thorough medical and sexual history is crucial in determining whether erectile dysfunction is organic, psychogenic, or multifactorial. This includes evaluating nocturnal erections to differentiate between physiological and psychogenic causes.
keyPoints: Complete medical and sexual histories are essential for assessing ED cause.
Nocturnal erections indicate intact neurologic and circulatory systems.
Organic ED is characterized by gradual changes in erection rigidity.

items.title: Link Between ED and Cardiovascular Disease
items.content: Erectile dysfunction can be an early indicator of cardiovascular disease, as it often precedes symptomatic coronary issues. Men with ED have higher rates of myocardial infarction.
keyPoints: ED may precede symptomatic coronary disease by several years.
Men with ED report higher rates of overt and silent myocardial infarction.
ED is an independent risk factor for cardiovascular disease.

items.title: When to Measure Serum Prolactin in Hypogonadal Men with ED
items.content: In hypogonadal men presenting with decreased libido or erectile dysfunction, measuring serum prolactin is recommended due to the possibility of a prolactinoma or other mass lesion at the sella turcica.
keyPoints: Serum prolactin measurement is indicated in hypogonadal men with ED for potential diagnosis of prolactinoma.
Low serum testosterone warrants further testing with gonadotropins to differentiate between primary and secondary hypogonadism.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · Erectile Dysfunction (part 2) | PATIENT EDUCATION

items.title: Role of Lifestyle Modifications in Managing ED
items.content: Lifestyle modifications such as diet changes, increased physical activity, and addressing high-risk behaviors can improve erectile function and overall health.
keyPoints: Counseling on lifestyle changes is crucial for men with comorbidities affecting erectile function.
High-risk lifestyle factors like obesity, smoking, alcohol abuse, and recreational drug use should be addressed as they contribute to ED.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · Erectile Dysfunction (part 2) | PATIENT EDUCATION

items.title: Understanding PDE-5 Inhibitors for ED Treatment
items.content: PDE-5 inhibitors like sildenafil, tadalafil, vardenafil, and avanafil are effective oral agents for erectile dysfunction (ED) across various etiologies. They work by inhibiting phosphodiesterase type 5 in the penis to enhance erections post sexual stimulation.
keyPoints: PDE-5 inhibitors include sildenafil, tadalafil, vardenafil, and avanafil.
Onset of action is approximately 30–120 minutes.
Consider reduced doses for elderly patients or those on medications like alpha blockers or CYP3A4 inhibitors (e.g., erythromycin, cimetidine).
Potential cardiovascular protective effects observed in studies.

items.title: Contraindications and Side Effects of PDE-5 Inhibitors
items.content: PDE-5 inhibitors are contraindicated for men on nitrate therapy due to the risk of severe hypotension. They should also be avoided in patients with congestive heart failure or cardiomyopathy. Common side effects include headaches, facial flushing, dyspepsia, and nasal congestion.
keyPoints: Contraindicated with nitrates (e.g., oral, sublingual) due to hypotensive risk.
Avoid in patients with congestive heart failure or cardiomyopathy.
Side effects: headaches (19%), facial flushing (9%), dyspepsia (6%), nasal congestion (4%).
Transient altered color vision reported with sildenafil; loin pain with tadalafil.

items.title: Differences and Considerations Among PDE-5 Inhibitors
items.content: While all PDE-5 inhibitors share a common mechanism, differences exist in onset of action and half-life. Tadalafil has the longest half-life; avanafil has the fastest onset. No compelling evidence supports one being superior over others.
keyPoints: Tadalafil has a longer half-life compared to other PDE-5 inhibitors.
Avanafil has the fastest onset of action among PDE-5 inhibitors.
No clinically relevant superiority among the four agents.

items.title: Combination of PDE-5i and Testosterone Therapy for ED in TD Patients
items.content: Men with erectile dysfunction (ED) and testosterone deficiency (TD) may experience enhanced efficacy when using phosphodiesterase type 5 inhibitors (PDE-5is) if combined with testosterone therapy. However, androgen supplementation is not recommended for men with normal testosterone levels due to the lack of benefit and increased risk of toxicity.
keyPoints: PDE-5is may be more effective when combined with testosterone therapy in TD patients.
Androgen supplementation without deficiency does not improve ED and poses additional risks.

items.title: Monitoring and Risks Associated with Testosterone Replacement Therapy
items.content: Testosterone replacement therapy carries potential risks such as thromboembolic events, water retention in heart failure patients, worsening sleep apnea, gynecomastia, and more. It is crucial to monitor testosterone levels, erectile function, and adverse effects regularly after initiating therapy.
keyPoints: FDA warning of a 'weak signal' for increased risk of thromboembolic events with testosterone therapy.
Regular reevaluation every 3–6 months and annually thereafter is necessary.
Adverse effects to monitor include gynecomastia, sleep apnea, LUTS/BPH, prostate cancer, lowering of HDL, erythrocytosis, liver function test elevations.

items.subtopic: VACUUM CONSTRICTION DEVICES | INTRAURETHRAL ALPROSTADIL | INTRACAVERNOSAL SELF-INJECTION | SURGERY
items.title: Non-Invasive and Minimally Invasive Options for Erectile Dysfunction (ED)
items.content: Vacuum constriction devices (VCDs) offer a non-invasive treatment for ED, suitable for patients who cannot take PDE-5 inhibitors or prefer not to use other interventions. They work by drawing venous blood into the penis and using a ring to maintain erection. Intraurethral alprostadil is an alternative when oral agents fail, with about 65% of men responding in-office but less than 50% achieving successful intercourse at home.
keyPoints: VCDs are non-invasive and suitable for patients unable or unwilling to use PDE-5 inhibitors.
Adverse events from VCDs include pain, numbness, bruising, and altered ejaculation.
Intraurethral alprostadil doses range from 125–1000 µg, with a 65% in-office response rate but <50% success at home.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · VACUUM CONSTRICTION DEVICES | INTRAURETHRAL ALPROSTADIL | INTRACAVERNOSAL SELF-INJECTION | SURGERY

items.subtopic: VACUUM CONSTRICTION DEVICES | INTRAURETHRAL ALPROSTADIL | INTRACAVERNOSAL SELF-INJECTION | SURGERY
items.title: Intracavernosal Injection and Surgical Options for ED
items.content: Intracavernosal injection of alprostadil is effective in 70–80% of patients with ED, though discontinuation rates are high due to its invasive nature. It's contraindicated in men at risk for priapism or hypersensitivity to the drug. Surgical implantation of penile prostheses offers a permanent solution for those refractory to medical treatments, with high satisfaction despite requiring surgery.
keyPoints: Intracavernosal alprostadil effective in 70–80% of patients; doses range from 1 to 40 µg.
Contraindications include hypersensitivity and risk of priapism (e.g., hypercoagulable states, sickle cell disease).
Penile prostheses offer high satisfaction rates but are considered after less invasive options.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · VACUUM CONSTRICTION DEVICES | INTRAURETHRAL ALPROSTADIL | INTRACAVERNOSAL SELF-INJECTION | SURGERY

items.subtopic: SEX THERAPY | FEMALE SEXUAL DYSFUNCTION | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Importance of Including Partners in Sex Therapy for Female Sexual Dysfunction
items.content: Sex therapy can be beneficial for addressing psychogenic or social factors affecting sexual dysfunction, especially when both partners are involved if the patient is in a relationship.
keyPoints: Sex therapy may help with psychogenic or social components of female sexual dysfunction (FSD).
Involving both partners in therapy is preferable if the patient has an ongoing relationship.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · SEX THERAPY | FEMALE SEXUAL DYSFUNCTION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PHYSIOLOGY OF THE FEMALE SEXUAL RESPONSE

items.subtopic: SEX THERAPY | FEMALE SEXUAL DYSFUNCTION | PHYSIOLOGY OF THE FEMALE SEXUAL RESPONSE
items.title: Role of Estrogens and Androgens in Female Sexual Response
items.content: Estrogens are crucial for normal female sexual response, with a likely but less established role for androgens. Both hormones work synergistically to enhance arousal.
keyPoints: Normal female sexual response requires estrogens.
Androgens may also play a role in enhancing sexual arousal.
Estrogens and androgens work together in the CNS to enhance sexual arousal.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · SEX THERAPY | FEMALE SEXUAL DYSFUNCTION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PHYSIOLOGY OF THE FEMALE SEXUAL RESPONSE

items.title: Approach to Evaluating Female Sexual Dysfunction (FSD)
items.content: A comprehensive evaluation of FSD should include medical, surgical, obstetric, psychological, gynecologic, sexual, and social histories. It is essential to assess for underlying medical disorders such as diabetes, cardiovascular disease, depression, anxiety, and neurologic diseases that may affect sexual health. Medications impacting arousal, libido, or orgasm must be reviewed. Physical examination should include genitalia assessment and pelvic floor evaluation.
keyPoints: Include comprehensive history: medical, surgical, obstetric, psychological, gynecologic, sexual, social
Assess for disorders affecting sexual health: diabetes, cardiovascular disease, depression, anxiety, neurologic diseases
Review medications impacting arousal, libido, orgasm
Physical examination should include genitalia and pelvic floor assessment

items.title: Diagnostic Classification of Female Sexual Dysfunction (FSD)
items.content: The newer classification of FSD is based on four components: hypoactive sexual desire, sexual interest arousal disorder, orgasmic disorder, and sexual pain disorder. Each component requires the presence of 'personal distress' for a diagnosis. This framework aids in identifying organic etiologies alongside psychosocial factors.
keyPoints: Four diagnostic components: hypoactive sexual desire, sexual interest arousal disorder, orgasmic disorder, sexual pain disorder
Personal distress is required for dysfunction diagnosis
Framework helps identify both organic and psychosocial causes

items.title: Role of Estrogen Replacement Therapy in Postmenopausal Women with Vaginal Atrophy
items.content: Estrogen replacement therapy can be beneficial for postmenopausal women experiencing vaginal atrophy, coital pain, and decreased clitoral sensitivity. Various estrogen preparations are available, including conjugated equine estrogens, estradiol vaginal cream, a sustained-release intravaginal estradiol ring, or low-dose estradiol tablets.
keyPoints: Estrogen replacement therapy can treat vaginal atrophy and improve clitoral sensitivity in postmenopausal women.
Available estrogen preparations include conjugated equine estrogens, estradiol vaginal cream, a sustained-release intravaginal estradiol ring, or low-dose estradiol tablet.
Vaginal estrogen preparations with the lowest systemic absorption rate are preferred for women with a history of breast cancer and severe vaginal atrophy.

items.title: Alternatives to Estrogen Therapy in Treating Atrophic Vaginitis
items.content: For postmenopausal women who are hesitant or unable to use estrogen-based treatments due to health concerns, such as a history of breast cancer or endometrial cancer, hyaluronic acid vaginal gel is an effective alternative for treating atrophic vaginitis.
keyPoints: Hyaluronic acid vaginal gel is efficacious in treating atrophic vaginitis.
It serves as an alternative to estrogen-based treatments, particularly for women with a history of breast cancer or endometrial cancer.

items.title: Flibanserin: Mechanism of Action and Clinical Use in HSDD
items.content: Flibanserin, approved for low sexual desire in premenopausal women with HSDD, acts as a 5-HT1A receptor agonist and 5-HT2A receptor antagonist. It modulates neurotransmitter release, increasing dopamine and norepinephrine while reducing serotonin. The drug should be discontinued if no improvement is seen after 8 weeks.
keyPoints: Approved for premenopausal women with HSDD
Acts as a full agonist at postsynaptic 5-HT1A receptors and antagonist at 5-HT2A receptors
Causes downstream release of dopamine and norepinephrine, reduction of serotonin
Discontinue if no improvement after 8 weeks
Serious side effects include low blood pressure and dizziness, especially with alcohol
Common adverse events: nausea, fatigue, sleepiness, insomnia
Requires REMS certification for healthcare providers
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · ORAL AGENTS | CLITORAL VACUUM DEVICE

items.title: Clitoral Vacuum Device as a Non-Pharmacologic Option for Arousal and Orgasmic Difficulties
items.content: A clitoral vacuum device can be used in patients with arousal and orgasmic difficulties. It is a handheld, battery-operated device that increases cavernosal blood flow, engorgement, and vaginal lubrication by applying a vacuum over the clitoris.
keyPoints: Used for arousal and orgasmic difficulties
Handheld, battery-operated device with a soft plastic cup
Applies vacuum to increase blood flow and engorgement
Promotes vaginal lubrication
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · ORAL AGENTS | CLITORAL VACUUM DEVICE

items.question: What is the estimated prevalence of male sexual dysfunction among middle-aged and elderly men?
items.explanation: Male sexual dysfunction affects up to 31% of middle-aged and elderly men.

items.question: Which gender has a higher prevalence of sexual dysfunction according to the passage?
items.explanation: Female sexual dysfunction has a higher prevalence (43%) than male sexual dysfunction.

items.question: What should physicians do to address the issue of sexual dysfunction during clinical encounters?
options: Avoid discussing it unless patients bring it up
Address it only in those with cardiovascular risk factors
Directly ask about sexual health as part of routine history
Refer all cases to a specialist
items.explanation: Physicians should directly address sexual health and make it a routine part of the clinical encounter.

items.question: Which neurotransmitter is primarily responsible for inducing vascular relaxation and promoting erection in males?
items.explanation: Nitric oxide induces vascular relaxation, which promotes erection.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · PHYSIOLOGY OF MALE SEXUAL RESPONSE

items.question: What is the role of phosphodiesterase type 5 (PDE-5) inhibitors in male sexual function?
options: They initiate the production of nitric oxide.
They reduce the breakdown of cyclic GMP, maintaining erections.
They increase the synthesis of endothelin-1.
They stimulate the sympathetic nervous system for ejaculation.
items.explanation: PDE-5 inhibitors maintain erections by reducing the breakdown of cyclic GMP.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · PHYSIOLOGY OF MALE SEXUAL RESPONSE

items.question: Which spinal segments are involved in parasympathetic innervation to the penis?
items.explanation: Parasympathetic nerve fibers to the penis arise from neurons in the S2-S4 sacral spinal segments.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · PHYSIOLOGY OF MALE SEXUAL RESPONSE

items.question: What is a common cause of retrograde ejaculation (RE) in men?
options: Failure of the internal urethral sphincter to close
Overactivity of the sympathetic nervous system
items.explanation: Retrograde ejaculation occurs when the internal urethral sphincter does not close.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · PHYSIOLOGY OF MALE SEXUAL RESPONSE

items.question: According to the Massachusetts Male Aging Study, what percentage of men aged 40–70 reported some degree of erectile dysfunction?
items.explanation: In the MMAS, 52% of responders reported some degree of ED.

items.question: Which medical condition is NOT mentioned as being associated with a higher incidence of erectile dysfunction?
items.explanation: The passage mentions diabetes mellitus, rheumatoid arthritis, and heart disease as associated with ED but not hyperthyroidism.

items.question: What is the most common organic cause of erectile dysfunction?
items.explanation: The passage states that vasculogenic disturbances are the most common organic cause.

items.question: Which of the following is a risk factor for erectile dysfunction?
items.explanation: The passage identifies smoking as a significant risk factor for ED.

items.question: Which of the following mechanisms contributes to psychogenic erectile dysfunction by inhibiting reflexogenic responses?
options: Psychogenic stimuli to the sacral cord
Excess sympathetic stimulation in an anxious man
items.explanation: Psychogenic stimuli to the sacral cord may inhibit reflexogenic responses, blocking activation of vasodilator outflow.

items.question: Which class of antihypertensive agents is most frequently implicated in medication-induced erectile dysfunction?
items.explanation: Thiazide diuretics and beta blockers are most frequently implicated in medication-induced erectile dysfunction.

items.question: Which antidepressant is associated with the highest risk of sexual dysfunction?
items.explanation: Paroxetine and escitalopram have been associated with the highest risk of sexual dysfunction among SSRIs.

items.question: Which validated questionnaire is mentioned as a more easily administered abridged version to assess erectile dysfunction?
items.explanation: The SHIM is an abridged version of the IIEF used to assess erectile dysfunction.

items.question: What type of erections occur during rapid eye movement (REM) sleep and require intact neurologic and circulatory systems?
items.explanation: Nocturnal erections occur during REM sleep and indicate the integrity of neurologic and circulatory systems.

items.question: What is a common reason for underreporting erectile dysfunction by patients?
items.explanation: Patient embarrassment and perceptions of physician inattention are major reasons for underreporting ED.

items.question: Which disorder is often associated with erectile dysfunction, warranting an assessment of lower urinary tract symptoms?
items.explanation: Men with ED often have concomitant LUTS from BPH, necessitating an assessment of these symptoms.

items.question: In hypogonadal men presenting with decreased libido or ED, which condition should be considered and investigated by measuring serum prolactin levels?
options: Prolactinoma or another mass lesion of the sella
items.explanation: Hyperprolactinemia can present with decreased libido and/or ED, suggesting a prolactinoma or another mass lesion of the sella.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · Erectile Dysfunction (part 2) | PATIENT EDUCATION

items.question: Which diagnostic test is NOT typically necessary in the initial evaluation of erectile dysfunction?
options: Nocturnal penile tumescence and rigidity studies
Vascular testing with penile Doppler ultrasound
items.explanation: Additional diagnostic testing is rarely necessary in the evaluation of ED, making nocturnal penile tumescence and rigidity studies optional.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · Erectile Dysfunction (part 2) | PATIENT EDUCATION

items.question: Which lifestyle modification can potentially improve erectile function by addressing underlying risk factors?
items.explanation: Lifestyle modifications such as smoking cessation are recommended to improve overall health and erectile function.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · Erectile Dysfunction (part 2) | PATIENT EDUCATION

items.question: Which of the following medications is NOT an approved oral agent for the treatment of erectile dysfunction (ED)?
items.explanation: All listed options are approved oral agents for ED; no incorrect option is provided.

items.question: Which PDE-5 inhibitor has the longest half-life among those discussed?
items.explanation: Tadalafil is unique in its longer half-life.

items.question: Which of the following side effects is specifically associated with tadalafil?
items.explanation: Approximately 6% of men taking tadalafil may experience loin pain.

items.question: Which condition is a contraindication for the use of PDE-5 inhibitors?
items.explanation: PDE-5is should be avoided in patients with congestive heart failure and cardiomyopathy.

items.question: Which of the following is a reason why androgen supplementation in men with normal testosterone levels is discouraged?
options: It increases the risk of thromboembolic events.
It does not provide benefit but adds additional risk for toxicity.
It improves insulin sensitivity without any risks.
It decreases the risk of cardiovascular events.
items.explanation: Androgen supplementation in men with normal testosterone levels is discouraged due to additional risk for toxicity without benefit.

items.question: What should be considered when evaluating the effectiveness of PDE-5 inhibitors (PDE-5is) for erectile dysfunction (ED) in men with testosterone deficiency?
options: PDE-5is are more effective without any additional therapy.
Combining PDE-5is with testosterone therapy may enhance their efficacy.
Testosterone replacement should be avoided entirely.
PDE-5is alone are sufficient for all patients with ED.
items.explanation: Men with ED and testosterone deficiency considering ED treatment with a PDE-5i should be informed that PDE-5is may be more effective if combined with testosterone therapy.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT recommended for men with secondary hypogonadism who desire fertility?
options: Selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) clomiphene citrate.
items.explanation: Men with secondary hypogonadism who desire fertility should not be treated directly with testosterone, but with an alternative such as the SERM clomiphene citrate.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT typically included in the comprehensive evaluation of female sexual dysfunction?
items.explanation: Clitoral Doppler ultrasonography is not typically included due to its uncertain utility and need for expensive equipment.

items.question: Which hormone should be considered in laboratory studies as it reflects adrenal androgen secretion?
items.explanation: Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA) reflects adrenal androgen secretion.

items.question: What is emphasized as a requirement for diagnosing female sexual dysfunction in the newer classification?
items.explanation: The newer classification emphasizes 'personal distress' as a requirement for diagnosing female sexual dysfunction.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT recommended as a first step in managing sexual dysfunction?
options: Lifestyle changes involving known risk factors
items.explanation: Behavioral modification, patient counseling, and lifestyle changes are recommended as first steps before considering hormonal therapy.

items.question: In postmenopausal women with a history of breast cancer, which estrogen preparation is preferred?
items.explanation: The lowest systemic absorption rate is preferred, which is often associated with the low-dose estradiol tablet.

items.question: What treatment has been found efficacious for atrophic vaginitis in women hesitant to use estrogen-based treatments?
items.explanation: Hyaluronic acid vaginal gel is efficacious for atrophic vaginitis in women who avoid estrogen-based treatments.

items.question: Which of the following best describes the mechanism of action of flibanserin in treating HSDD?
options: Flibanserin acts as a full agonist at postsynaptic 5-HT1A receptors and an antagonist at postsynaptic 5-HT2A receptors.
Flibanserin is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI).
Flibanserin increases dopamine levels by acting on dopamine receptors directly.
Flibanserin works by blocking norepinephrine reuptake.
items.explanation: Flibanserin acts as a full agonist at postsynaptic 5-HT1A receptors and an antagonist at postsynaptic 5-HT2A receptors, which differentiates it from other agents like SSRIs.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · ORAL AGENTS | CLITORAL VACUUM DEVICE

items.question: Which of the following is a potential serious side effect of flibanserin that necessitates discontinuation if not improved after 8 weeks?
items.explanation: Flibanserin may cause potentially serious side effects like low blood pressure and dizziness, especially when mixed with alcohol.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · ORAL AGENTS | CLITORAL VACUUM DEVICE

items.question: What is the primary action of a clitoral vacuum device in patients with arousal and orgasmic difficulties?
options: It applies a vacuum over the stimulated clitoris to enhance blood flow.
It acts as an oral agent to increase sexual desire.
items.explanation: The device works by applying a vacuum over the stimulated clitoris, causing increased cavernosal blood flow and engorgement.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · ORAL AGENTS | CLITORAL VACUUM DEVICE

items.statement: Female sexual dysfunction has been studied more intensely than male sexual dysfunction.
items.explanation: The passage states that female sexual dysfunction, although having a higher prevalence, has been studied less intensely.

items.statement: Physicians should routinely address sexual health in patients with cardiovascular risk factors.
items.explanation: The passage suggests that addressing sexual health should be routine, particularly in those with cardiovascular risk factors.

items.statement: Priapism is characterized by a non-painful, persistent erection.
items.explanation: Priapism refers to a persistent and painful erection.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · PHYSIOLOGY OF MALE SEXUAL RESPONSE

items.statement: Detumescence is mediated by norepinephrine from the sympathetic nerves.
items.explanation: Norepinephrine from the sympathetic nerves mediates detumescence.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · PHYSIOLOGY OF MALE SEXUAL RESPONSE

items.statement: Erectile dysfunction is considered a normal part of the aging process.
items.explanation: The passage states that ED is not considered a normal part of aging.

items.statement: Diabetic men have an incidence rate of erectile dysfunction between 35–75%.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that diabetic ED occurs in 35–75% of men with diabetes mellitus.

items.statement: Calcium channel blockers are cited more frequently than beta blockers in causing medication-induced erectile dysfunction.
items.explanation: Beta blockers and thiazide diuretics are implicated most frequently, not calcium channel blockers.

items.statement: SSRIs inhibit nitric oxide synthase, contributing to erectile dysfunction.
items.explanation: SSRIs have been shown to be potent inhibitors of nitric oxide synthase.

items.statement: Ejaculation issues are more commonly affected than erection problems in erectile dysfunction.
items.explanation: Erection is much more commonly affected than ejaculation in cases of erectile dysfunction.

items.statement: Men with erectile dysfunction are at increased risk for cardiovascular diseases.
items.explanation: ED is associated with higher rates of myocardial infarction and can precede symptomatic coronary disease.

items.statement: Serum chemistries, hemoglobin A1c, and lipid profiles can provide evidence of systemic diseases associated with ED.
items.explanation: These tests can reveal diabetes, hyperlipidemia, or other conditions linked to erectile dysfunction.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · Erectile Dysfunction (part 2) | PATIENT EDUCATION

items.statement: Psychological diagnostic tests are mandatory for all patients with ED.
items.explanation: Psychological testing is optional and used selectively to understand pathologic mechanisms of ED.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · Erectile Dysfunction (part 2) | PATIENT EDUCATION

items.statement: PDE-5 inhibitors are contraindicated in men receiving nitrate therapy for cardiovascular disease.
items.explanation: PDE-5is can potentiate the hypotensive effect of nitrates, making them contraindicated.

items.statement: Sildenafil is associated with a clinically significant risk of nonarteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (NAION).
items.explanation: Data supporting the association between PDE-5is and NAION are limited, but caution is advised.

items.statement: Testosterone therapy has no known risks and is safe for all patients.
items.explanation: Testosterone therapy has known risks, such as water retention in heart failure patients and worsening sleep apnea.

items.statement: SHBG-bound testosterone is bioavailable and can bind to the androgen receptor.
items.explanation: Testosterone bound to SHBG does not bind to the androgen receptor and is not bioavailable.

items.subtopic: VACUUM CONSTRICTION DEVICES | INTRAURETHRAL ALPROSTADIL | INTRACAVERNOSAL SELF-INJECTION | SURGERY
items.statement: Intracavernosal self-injection therapy is contraindicated in men with hypercoagulable states.
items.explanation: Injection therapy is contraindicated in men at risk for priapism, including those with hypercoagulable states.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · VACUUM CONSTRICTION DEVICES | INTRAURETHRAL ALPROSTADIL | INTRACAVERNOSAL SELF-INJECTION | SURGERY

items.subtopic: VACUUM CONSTRICTION DEVICES | INTRAURETHRAL ALPROSTADIL | INTRACAVERNOSAL SELF-INJECTION | SURGERY
items.statement: Penile prostheses are associated with high complication rates.
items.explanation: Penile prostheses are associated with a low rate of complications.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · VACUUM CONSTRICTION DEVICES | INTRAURETHRAL ALPROSTADIL | INTRACAVERNOSAL SELF-INJECTION | SURGERY

items.subtopic: SEX THERAPY | FEMALE SEXUAL DYSFUNCTION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PHYSIOLOGY OF THE FEMALE SEXUAL RESPONSE
items.statement: Female sexual dysfunction (FSD) is more commonly detected and treated than male sexual dysfunctions.
items.explanation: The passage states that FSDs are substantially undetected by clinicians and undertreated even when recognized.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · SEX THERAPY | FEMALE SEXUAL DYSFUNCTION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PHYSIOLOGY OF THE FEMALE SEXUAL RESPONSE

items.subtopic: SEX THERAPY | FEMALE SEXUAL DYSFUNCTION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PHYSIOLOGY OF THE FEMALE SEXUAL RESPONSE
items.statement: Vaginal lubrication is a transudate of serum resulting from increased pelvic blood flow associated with arousal.
items.explanation: The passage describes vaginal lubrication as a transudate of serum due to increased pelvic blood flow during arousal.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · SEX THERAPY | FEMALE SEXUAL DYSFUNCTION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PHYSIOLOGY OF THE FEMALE SEXUAL RESPONSE

items.statement: A comprehensive evaluation of female sexual dysfunction should include limited laboratory testing.
items.explanation: Limited laboratory testing is part of the comprehensive evaluation for female sexual dysfunction.

items.statement: The physical examination in evaluating FSD does not need to assess genitalia including the clitoris.
items.explanation: The physical examination should assess the genitalia, including the clitoris.

items.statement: Low levels of testosterone or DHEA are effective predictors of a positive therapeutic outcome with androgen therapy.
items.explanation: The passage states that low levels of these hormones do not predict a positive outcome with androgen therapy.

items.statement: Vaginal lubricants and moisturizers are less effective than local estrogen therapy for treating atrophic vaginitis.
items.explanation: The passage states that vaginal lubricants and moisturizers have comparable efficacy to local estrogen therapy.

items.statement: Flibanserin should be discontinued if there is no improvement in sex drive after 8 weeks.
items.explanation: The passage states that flibanserin should be discontinued if there is no improvement in sex drive after 8 weeks.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · ORAL AGENTS | CLITORAL VACUUM DEVICE

items.statement: Bremelanotide has clinically significant interactions with ethanol.
items.explanation: The passage specifies that bremelanotide has no clinically significant interactions with ethanol.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · ORAL AGENTS | CLITORAL VACUUM DEVICE

items.assertion: Premature ejaculation is often related to anxiety or learned behavior.
items.reason: It can be treated with behavioral therapy or SSRIs.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · PHYSIOLOGY OF MALE SEXUAL RESPONSE

items.assertion: Venous leak causes premature detumescence due to a specific anatomic defect.
items.reason: It is caused by insufficient relaxation of the corporal smooth muscle.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; venous leak is not due to a specific anatomic defect.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · PHYSIOLOGY OF MALE SEXUAL RESPONSE

items.assertion: Erectile dysfunction can be a sentinel symptom for cardiovascular disease.
items.reason: ED and cardiovascular diseases share etiologies such as endothelial dysfunction.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Androgen replacement therapy is useful for all cases of erectile dysfunction.
items.reason: Normal levels of testosterone are important for optimizing nitric oxide synthase and the nitric oxide cascade.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; androgen replacement is not useful when endogenous testosterone levels are normal.

items.assertion: Nocturnal erections are useful for distinguishing between physiological and psychogenic erectile dysfunction.
items.reason: Nocturnal erections require intact neurologic and circulatory systems, indicating the absence of organic causes.
items.explanation: The presence or absence of nocturnal erections helps differentiate physiological from psychogenic ED.

items.assertion: Situational erectile dysfunction suggests an organic cause.
items.reason: Situational ED arises with new or extramarital relationships, indicating a psychological component.
items.explanation: Situational ED is more indicative of psychogenic causes rather than organic ones.

items.assertion: PDE-5 inhibitors may have a cardiovascular protective effect.
items.reason: Studies suggest that men with ED taking PDE-5is have lower rates of major adverse cardiovascular events and overall mortality.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Tadalafil has a faster onset of action compared to other PDE-5 inhibitors.
items.reason: Avanafil appears to have the fastest onset of action among the PDE-5 inhibitors.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; avanafil, not tadalafil, has the fastest onset.

items.subtopic: VACUUM CONSTRICTION DEVICES | INTRAURETHRAL ALPROSTADIL | INTRACAVERNOSAL SELF-INJECTION | SURGERY
items.assertion: Penile prostheses are considered after less invasive options for ED treatment.
items.reason: Because of the permanence of prosthetic devices, patients should first consider less invasive options.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Sexual Dysfunction · VACUUM CONSTRICTION DEVICES | INTRAURETHRAL ALPROSTADIL | INTRACAVERNOSAL SELF-INJECTION | SURGERY
